text: Multi-site da Vinci incisional hernia repair using primary closure by Dr. Clark Gerhart. Numerous methods have been used to close incisional hernias. The standard laparoscopic approach is to perform a mesh patch type closure, securing the mesh with either trans-fascial sutures or tacks or a combination of both. This technique, however, typically leaves the fascial defect open, which may result in mesh migrations, seromas, or bulging at the hernia repair. Primary closure is a difficult procedure using standard laparoscopic techniques and is rarely used. The advent of robotic surgery, however, allows for more precise manipulation of laparoscopic needle holders, which now allows for primary closure of the fascial defect. Here we show a incisional hernia repair accomplished with primary sutured closure.

The surgeon uses a non-absorbable polypropylene V-lock suture to close the fascial defect. The suture's barbs maintain tension along the suture line, and its looped end facilitates starting the stitch without knot tying. The robotic instruments' wristed articulation enables precise needle placement through the fascia, while the trailing arm maintains tension on the suture line.

The surgeon uses adequate sized needles with the V-lock suture to take generous bites of the fascia, advancing the suture to close the fascial defect. The barbs on the suture help maintain tension, ensuring the defect remains closed as the suture line progresses along the defect until it is completely closed.

The surgeon releases carbon dioxide to decrease intra-abdominal pressure, facilitating the approximation of the margins of a large fascial defect. The hernia is located along the lateral edge of previously placed mesh, which is reincorporated into the current repair.

The surgeon uses wrist articulation of robotic needle holders to place the suture at various angles, tightening the suture line to prevent gapping between passes. The suture line extends beyond the apex of the fascial defect, then turns back on itself for additional passes through the fascia. The barbs on the V-lock suture provide resistance, eliminating the need for a knot at the end.
The surgeon completes the primary closure of an incisional hernia using a multi-site robotic technique. The suture is divided, and the needle is carefully removed from the abdominal cavity.
